data_in.description: Preprocessed spectra (peak-filtered or reference spectral library in mzML, mzXML, msp, MGF, or JSON format)

data_out.description: Pairwise cosine similarity scores matrix file (CSV or pickle format with spectrum identifiers and similarity scores)

domain_knowledge: Cosine similarity is a standard pairwise metric for comparing mass spectra, measuring the angle between vectorized peak intensity profiles to quantify spectral overlap.
Matchms supports multiple input formats (mzML, mzXML, msp, MGF, JSON); the input spectrum file must be in one of these supported formats to ensure successful loading.
A scores matrix is a symmetric N×N table where N is the number of input spectra, with each cell [i,j] containing the cosine similarity score between spectrum i and spectrum j (typically ranging 0–1).
Matchms enables large-scale spectral similarity comparisons, making it suitable for high-throughput pairwise scoring across libraries of hundreds or thousands of spectra.

direct_checks: verify that a Python script using matchms.similarity.cosine can be executed on the input spectra file without errors
verify that the output scores matrix file exists and has .npy, .csv, or .pkl format
verify that the scores matrix has shape (N, N) where N equals the number of spectra in the input file
verify that all values in the scores matrix are in the range [0.0, 1.0] (cosine similarity bounds)
verify that the scores matrix is symmetric (i.e., scores[i,j] == scores[j,i] for all i,j)
verify that the diagonal of the scores matrix equals 1.0 (self-similarity), byte-for-byte or within numerical precision (±1e-6)

script_runs: execute a minimal matchms cosine similarity workflow (import Spectrum, CosineGreedy/CosineHungarian, compute pairwise similarities) on reference spectra from matchms repository without runtime errors
expert_review: assess whether the cosine similarity implementation correctly weights peak intensities and mass-to-charge ratios according to the Cosine/CosineGreedy algorithm specification
assess whether the choice of cosine variant (CosineGreedy, CosineHungarian, or ModifiedCosine) is justified and documented for the use case
assess whether preprocessing/normalization of input spectra (if applied before cosine scoring) is appropriate and does not artificially inflate or suppress similarity values

cmd: import matchms; from matchms.importing import load_spectra; from matchms.similarity import CosineGreedy; import pandas as pd; spectra = list(load_spectra('{inputs.preprocessed_spectra}')); scorer = CosineGreedy(); scores = [[scorer.pair(s1, s2) for s2 in spectra] for s1 in spectra]; df = pd.DataFrame(scores, index=[s.metadata.get('spectrum_id', f'spectrum_{i}') for i, s in enumerate(spectra)], columns=[s.metadata.get('spectrum_id', f'spectrum_{i}') for i, s in enumerate(spectra)]); df.to_csv('cosine_similarity_scores.csv'); print(f'Matrix shape: {df.shape}'); print(f'Score range: [{df.min().min():.4f}, {df.max().max():.4f}]')

workflow_description: 1. Load preprocessed spectra from the input file (peak-filtered spectra or reference spectral library) using matchms. 2. Apply the cosine similarity measure to compute pairwise scores between all spectrum pairs. 3. Construct a scores matrix with spectrum identifiers as row and column labels. 4. Save the scores matrix to a named output file in a structured format (CSV or pickle).
